title: The overlooked health impacts of extreme rainfall exposure in 30 East Asian cities
methods: subgroup stratification by age and period
sensitivity analysis with alternative precipitation datasets
data_sources: National death registries (Japan, South Korea, China, Taiwan, Hong Kong)

data_scope.city_breakdown: 17 mainland China (incl. Hong Kong), 7 South Korea, 7 Japan, 3 Taiwan
data_scope.mortality_data_access: restricted; available on request from corresponding author

core_conclusion: Only rare extreme rainfall events — those exceeding 5- or 10-year return-period thresholds — significantly elevate respiratory mortality in East Asian cities (RR 1.29 and 1.33, respectively). The effect peaks at lag 2 days and dissipates by lag 5, is strongest for asthma and COPD among the elderly, and is robust across alternative precipitation databases, lag specifications, and exclusion of concurrent flood or tropical cyclone events.
why_it_matters: Identifies extreme rainfall as an overlooked cause of chronic respiratory mortality beyond heat, cold, and air pollution; provides a defensible return-period trigger threshold for emergency health planning; quantifies sizable excess mortality (4.46 deaths per 10,000 per city for 5-year events); robust multi-country evidence strengthens causal inference.
why_nature: Cross-disciplinary bridge between climate science and epidemiology relevant to Nature Sustainability; focuses on densely populated, climate-vulnerable East Asian megacities; directly actionable for adaptation policy; methodologically novel IDF approach replicable globally.
added_value_to_my_research: Fine-resolution precipitation hazard surface (multi-source fusion) as covariate for spatiotemporal health models
SHAP-augmented DLNM RR estimates -> spatial-heterogeneity attribution of rainfall-mortality modifiers (GDP, green space)
Rainfall-adjusted R2SFCA travel-time degradation -> climate-induced healthcare access deficit index
Neighborhood-level DLNM disaggregation -> climate-health equity index using spatial entropy across vulnerability quintiles
City-adjacency physics-informed GNN with IDF exceedance as physical prior -> transferable climate-mortality early-warning model

related.title: Urban development pattern's influence on extreme rainfall occurrences
related.relation: links urbanization morphology to extreme rainfall patterns — extends the paper's exposure definition with sub-city spatial detail

related.title: Tens of thousands additional deaths annually in cities of China between 1.5 °C and 2.0 °C warming
related.relation: parallel multi-city mortality burden study in China under climate change — contextualizes excess-death magnitudes

related.title: Repeated and localized flooding is an underestimated challenge for urban disaster risk management
related.relation: addresses localized urban flood risk management — relevant to indirect pathway via healthcare access disruption

related.title: Socio-economic factors impact vulnerability to and burden of heat- and cold-related mortality in Europe
related.relation: socioeconomic disaggregation of temperature-mortality burden — methodological template for within-city equity extension

related.title: Precipitation disaster hotspots depend on historical climate variability
related.relation: historical climate variability and record precipitation risk — directly relevant to the IDF return-period framing

related.title: Economic valuation of temperature-related mortality attributed to urban heat islands in European cities
related.relation: economic mortality valuation from urban climate exposure — model for attaching cost estimates to rainfall-mortality excess deaths

figures.caption: Spatial locations of the cities experiencing extreme rainfall events with three different return periods (2, 5, and 10 years); colour encodes IDF-derived intensity threshold per city.
Overall lag structure (0-14 days) in effects of extreme rainfall days on daily respiratory mortality, by three different return periods.
Cumulative effects of 5-year return-period extreme rainfall on respiratory mortality over 14 lag days, stratified by disease (asthma, COPD, pneumonia), age group, and two study periods.
